metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Washington's death by bloodletting shows a 2,000-year failure to actually test treatment efficacy. The Neyman-Rubin framework defines individual effect as R_Ti minus R_Ci, but only one is ever observed. Comparability between treated and control groups is an assumption, not something proven. Explicitly non-comparable controls, like the EITC counterparts, break naive causal claims.

artifactLines: Washington's death by bloodletting shows a 2,000-year failure to actually test treatment efficacy.
The Neyman-Rubin framework defines individual effect as R_Ti minus R_Ci, but only one is ever observed.
Comparability between treated and control groups is an assumption, not something proven.
Explicitly non-comparable controls, like the EITC counterparts, break naive causal claims.

beats.narration_text: The Neyman-Rubin idea that individual i has effect R_Ti minus R_Ci but you only ever see one outcome is the crux I want to sit with. Using the George Washington bloodletting and PALM trial examples, help me feel the missing-data problem. Give me: (1) the fundamental problem of causal inference stated plainly, (2) why the EITC controls were called not comparable, (3) one design like the PALM randomization that recovers a credible counterfactual.
